title: Jianzhi symmetrical papercut
subtitle: The Chinese paper cutting tradition of folded symmetrical designs
excerpt: Jianzhi, the Chinese folk art of paper cutting, produces the characteristic red symmetrical designs seen at New Year celebrations and used as window decorations. The traditional method uses thin red paper, folded multiple times, cut with scissors or a craft knife, and unfolded to reveal radially symmetrical patterns. The designs draw on a vocabulary of auspicious symbols, fish, flowers, birds, characters meaning good fortune.
sourceNotes: Jianzhi is documented as a Chinese folk art tradition from the sixth century AD. The craft is a UNESCO-designated Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (2009). The techniques described here are based on the public-domain craft tradition as documented in multiple art history and craft education sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Chinese folk art of paper cutting. The name means 'cut paper'. The tradition dates from the sixth century and uses the cuts to produce auspicious symbols for celebration and decoration.
Symmetry arranged around a central point, so the design reads the same when rotated. Produced in paper cutting by folding the sheet four or more times before cutting.

A cut that removes the background and leaves the design shape intact. The opposite of a negative cut, which removes the design and leaves the surround. Traditional jianzhi uses both, often within the same piece.

content.text: through folding: a square of paper folded in half twice creates four layers; folded into eighths creates eight layers. Cuts through all layers simultaneously produce a design that repeats four or eight times around the centre when unfolded. The
approach, removing background and preserving the design, requires planning each cut so the design elements remain connected through the folded piece.

attrs.title: Craft knife safety
attrs.body: Cut away from fingers, keep the blade at a low angle to the mat, and replace blades when they drag rather than slice.

items.name: Thin red paper, 60 to 80 gsm (thinner paper cuts more cleanly through multiple layers)
Pencil for drawing the design on the folded sheet
Craft knife with a fine point blade (No. 11 or similar)
Small sharp scissors for outer edges and curves
White card or backing paper for mounting

content.text: Start with a 15 cm square of thin red paper. Fold in half diagonally (corner to corner) to make a triangle. Fold in half again along the new long edge to make a smaller triangle. Fold in half once more; the paper is now folded into eighths. The centre point of the original square is now at the apex of the folded triangle. All radial cuts will emerge from or connect to this apex.

Draw the design in pencil on the top face of the folded triangle. Traditional motifs on a beginner piece: a circle cut at the apex (creates a central flower centre when unfolded), petal shapes cut from the long edges (creates flower petals), and a small fish or leaf shape cut from the short edge (creates repeated motifs around the outer ring). Mark the areas to cut away, not the areas to keep. Every cut shape must be bounded by paper on at least one side that connects to the fold.

Cut interior shapes with the craft knife, working on the mat. Cut from smallest to largest shapes: small interior details first, then larger openings, then the outer silhouette edge last. Cutting the outer edge first can cause the folded stack to shift and misalign the inner cuts. Thin paper cuts cleanly through eight layers with a sharp blade; if the layers shift, use a small binder clip at the open edges to hold them together before cutting. Unfold gently after all cuts are complete.

items.symptom: The paper layers shift during cutting, misaligning the pattern
items.cause: The folds were not creased sharply enough, or the blade is dragging and pushing layers sideways.
items.fix: Crease all folds firmly with a fingernail. Clip the open edges of the folded stack with a binder clip outside the cutting area. Replace the blade to ensure it cuts rather than drags.

items.symptom: The design falls apart when unfolded because sections are disconnected
items.cause: A cut reached a fold edge at a point where no paper bridge remained to connect the sections.
items.fix: Before cutting, trace each proposed cut and check whether it will leave at least one continuous paper connection between all parts of the design. Add a small bridge (uncut strip of paper, 2 to 3 mm wide) wherever a connection is weak.
